This Court, under the strong basis standard, has suggested that a strong basis is met when the threshold conditions for liability are met. That's what this Court said in Bush v. Vera, a plurality in Bush v. Vera, as well as Abrams v. Johnson. The question is how to apply that standard to this case. That standard would suggest that a prima facie case, which, again, is not just adverse impact alone, but it's adverse impact that creates an inference of discrimination could be enough. Here we have not just that, not just--

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: Mr. Meade, let me -- let me go back to one earlier question. Suppose everybody agrees that you're right on the -- on the record here now, and the City goes ahead and does another test, with all the advantages and studies they've made and so forth and so on, and it turns out you just had an unfortunate selection of candidates, and they come out exactly the same way. Would you agree that at that time the City would have to certify the results?

turns.name: CHRISTOPHER J. MEADE
turns.text: --Assuming that it was a test that was valid--

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: It's a test they made after talking to everybody who testified in this case and filed amicus briefs and everything else ---- [Laughter] And they came out, and it turned out exactly the same results.

turns.name: CHRISTOPHER J. MEADE
turns.text: --Absolutely. If the Petitioners--

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: --I'm sorry--

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: Absolutely what? [Laughter]

turns.name: CHRISTOPHER J. MEADE
turns.text: --Absolutely yes.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Absolutely positively? [Laughter]

turns.name: CHRISTOPHER J. MEADE
turns.text: Absolutely positively.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: --I still -- I still don't have absolutely yes -- of what?

turns.name: CHRISTOPHER J. MEADE
turns.text: Yes, because--

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Yes, they can--

turns.name: CHRISTOPHER J. MEADE
turns.text: --Yes, they -- they need to certify the -- the results.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: They would have to certify it.

turns.name: CHRISTOPHER J. MEADE
turns.text: They would have to certify the results. Sorry I was unclear. They would have to certify the -- the results. The question here is whether there is in fact a fair process. It's--

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: --Well, just to get back to your answer to Justice Stevens, you say they'd have to certify it. You say that, in that situation, the decisionmaker could not have a reasonable basis for thinking further investigation is required. Why? Just because the second test came out the same way? It's not at all reasonable to think they ought to look at it further?

turns.name: CHRISTOPHER J. MEADE
turns.text: --Well, not on the basis of -- of the investigation that Justice Stevens, I understood, hypothesized, as part of the example.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: And that would be so, even if another Mr. Hornick showed up and said, I could ---- [Laughter] I could make a better -- I could make a test? And here are some problems with this second test that you gave?

turns.name: CHRISTOPHER J. MEADE
turns.text: Again, having gone through all the different examples that Justice Stevens said, at that point then -- then it would be -- the City would need to go forward with the test.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel.

turns.name: CHRISTOPHER J. MEADE
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Mr. Coleman, to keep the time even here, I think you have 8 minutes.

turns.name: GREGORY S. COLEMAN
turns.text: There's another statute that the Court ought to consider in the Title VII context, and that's section 2000e-7, which says that Title VII will not overrule and pre-empt State law unless there is a violation of Title VII. In asserting that, under any reasonable basis, as long as they have a reasonable basis, they can dispense with all the provisions of Connecticut civil service law, all these provisions that were put in place to get rid of cronyism, to get rid of discrimination can be set aside based on nothing more than a numerical disparity or perhaps a concern about the test we think cuts against Congress's intent in Title VII in respecting these State and local laws that are intended to ensure that employment practices are fair and that they choose and select those who are best qualified to put into these very important first-responder organizations.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Do you -- I'm still back on -- a university decides that tenure requirements lead to fewer women professors, so they say as an experiment what we would like to do is not have them for a couple of years; see what happens. On your view is that unconstitutional? Because, after all, it will certainly mean that certain majority race assistant professors have now lost the promotion they otherwise would have.

turns.name: GREGORY S. COLEMAN
turns.text: I think consistent with the answer I gave you before, Justice Breyer, that based on--

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: That it's unconstitutional?

turns.name: GREGORY S. COLEMAN
turns.text: --Based on that alone--

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: GREGORY S. COLEMAN
turns.text: --No, it would not be.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: It would not be unconstitutional?

turns.name: GREGORY S. COLEMAN
turns.text: It--

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: And what about--

turns.name: GREGORY S. COLEMAN
turns.text: --You're not taking away tenure from anybody.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: --All right. Oh, oh.

turns.name: GREGORY S. COLEMAN
turns.text: You're just saying we want to change the tenure process.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: But what we are doing is not giving the promotions to the assistant professors who otherwise would have job security.

turns.name: GREGORY S. COLEMAN
turns.text: The analogy to your analogy is that if we have a series of people who go through the tenure process that exists and it turns out, you know, we -- we don't like the results, and--

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Oh, so.

turns.name: GREGORY S. COLEMAN
turns.text: --therefore we want to change it.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: It's the result -- it's that you identify the person that makes your -- so in Texas, for example, they take the top 10 percent of all the high school graduates and put them in the university. Now, suppose they just decided, you know what we want to do? The top 5 percent. We want to see how that works. And, of course, then there are people who in fact would have gotten into the university -- and perhaps we can imagine a majority of the majority race -- and now they don't. Can Texas do that?

turns.name: GREGORY S. COLEMAN
turns.text: Well, you've chosen a very controversial subject.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: I know that, but I -- I ---- [Laughter] That was not my objective.

turns.name: GREGORY S. COLEMAN
turns.text: If--

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: I want to test out just what it is about this case.

turns.name: GREGORY S. COLEMAN
turns.text: --If -- if that is not done on the -- on the basis of race, then, no. The institution of the 10 percent rule itself, most people believe--

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Well, you said no. Can Texas do that or not?

turns.name: GREGORY S. COLEMAN
turns.text: --Likely, yes. The answer is--

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: GREGORY S. COLEMAN
turns.text: --it can do it.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Okay. And the difference here precisely is what?

turns.name: GREGORY S. COLEMAN
turns.text: Is that, under State law, these individuals had gone through an existing process and had -- under State law, had been determined to be the most qualified candidates and, barring anything else, would have been promoted. So the classification that is made clearly does distinguish between those who are qualified for promotion and those who are not qualified for promotion and would not receive. It violates that -- that singular principle of individual dignity to have these individuals be told, on the basis of race, you're not--

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: But if it were shown that, in fact, this test was not job-related and, in fact, the majority of fire departments scotched this test years ago and substituted what most agree is a better test, even so you would say it would violate the rights of the plaintiffs you represent, even--

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: --Even if there's strong evidence that it's not job-related and that there's a better test that doesn't produce these skewed results?

turns.name: GREGORY S. COLEMAN
turns.text: --I don't think that's what we've said, Justice Ginsburg. Under our alternative formulation in which the Court recognizes--

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: But what -- what would that do to the civil service merit system that says if you pass the test you should be certified?

turns.name: GREGORY S. COLEMAN
turns.text: --The difference is this, Justice Ginsburg: The example you have given would clearly satisfy or likely satisfy a strong basis in evidence that you are actually in violation of the disparate impact provision of Title VII. There are three prongs. The first is adverse impact; the second is that your test is not related; and the third is the existence of this alternative that is equally valid and that results in lower disparate impact. The City has never asserted -- and I hear it today continue to say, we don't have to show those other two prongs, that a numerical disparity enough may allow the City to conclude that there must be something wrong with the test. This kind of res ipsa loquitur theory of disparate impact is one that the courts have not recognized and that Watson said we cannot allow because it results in racial balancing and soft quotas based on disparate impact--

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: But in your -- in my example, to go back, the thing you've identified, it seems to me, is Texas couldn't do this. It couldn't look at the class that they're going to choose with the 10 percent and say, you know, there are not enough minorities, I think we'll go to 15 percent this year.

turns.name: GREGORY S. COLEMAN
turns.text: --That I agree with, Justice Breyer.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: It could not. And moreover, in the case of the tenure, what the school couldn't do is it couldn't say, looking over at the present tenured faculty and those who were just ready for promotion and who in all probability will be, we're going to go to the non-tenure system this year.

turns.name: GREGORY S. COLEMAN
turns.text: I also agree with that.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: That they couldn't do that. And again you that say the ordinary employer across America who announces a deadline for getting in applications cannot, once it sees those applications, say, you know, there are not enough minorities. I want to extend the deadline.

turns.name: GREGORY S. COLEMAN
turns.text: That's also correct, Justice Breyer.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: All right. And therefore this is a very far-reaching decision.

turns.name: GREGORY S. COLEMAN
turns.text: No, not necessarily, Your Honor. Okay, what--

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: You are -- you are saying, as I understand it, that if the -- if the city in a case like this, prior to giving a test, looked at the test and says, wait a minute, this is going to produce really disparate results, they can stop, regroup, and think it through again and maybe come up with a different test.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: But if they don't realize that and they go ahead with the test, and they then see the -- the disparate results, it's too late. And it seems to me that the trouble with drawing that distinction is that the city is not in the testing business. They are unlikely to know what the results are going to be. So you're saying that the city that is -- that is prescient can adjust, the city that doesn't find out there's something wrong or at least undesirable from their standpoint until after the test results cannot readjust?

turns.name: GREGORY S. COLEMAN
turns.text: --I don't think that's our position, Justice Souter. The first case I think is the hypothetical Justice Kennedy posed to me. The second case, as we've been talking about, is that you identify the disparate impact after the test has been given. Under this alternative theory that would allow a -- an employer to respond, all we are asking under the strong basis in evidence test is that you not react out of a concern, or out of this mere reaction to the numbers, but that you then look, is the test valid? Do you have convincing evidence, in the words of Wygant, to form a strong basis in evidence that if you did go forward--

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: But if they see it coming, they don't have to show a strong basis in evidence for changing the test prior to the time they give it?

turns.name: GREGORY S. COLEMAN
turns.text: --Consistent with what -- my conversation with Justice Breyer, if they see it coming and do it ahead of time, it doesn't violate that principle of individual dignity and that -- and doesn't discriminate against particularized and identifiable individuals.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. The case is submitted.
